_note: ORIGINAL content. Think C++ and the Google C++ Class outline informed coverage only; no prose, examples, or exercises were copied.
title: Debug: Part 2
summary: Find harder bugs involving object lifetime, container sizes, and changing state.
refresher: Harder C++ bugs often appear when code keeps an address too long, subtracts from an empty size, or changes a container while using a position inside it. Trace the first value or reference that becomes invalid.

blocks.body: Some C++ bugs come from values that were once valid but are no longer safe to use. A local object disappears when its function ends. A vector may move its elements when it grows. An empty container has no final element. Check these moments when a later line behaves strangely.

A pointer or reference that still names an object after that object is gone is called dangling. Positions into a vector can also become invalid after the vector changes its storage. Trace when the object was created, changed, and destroyed instead of looking only at the line where the crash appears.

blocks.title: What is std::size_t?
blocks.body: `std::size_t` is the nonnegative integer type C++ uses for sizes and indexes. You do not need to memorize its internal size. Match it with `.size()` and keep the index strictly below the container's size.

blocks.caption: The loop uses the vector's size as its stopping point and never allows the index to reach the size itself.
Do not return a reference to a local object that is about to disappear.

blocks.body: The local string exists only while `label` is running. Returning a reference would leave the caller with a name for an object that has already disappeared. Returning the string by value gives the caller its own valid result.

blocks.title: Check emptiness before subtracting from size
blocks.body: Container sizes use a nonnegative number type. Subtracting one from an empty size does not produce a useful negative index; it wraps to a very large value. Handle the empty case first.

blocks.prompt: Why is `i <= v.size()` unsafe for indexing?
blocks.why: The count itself is one past the final valid index, yet the inclusive test allows it.
An empty size cannot be safely decremented as an unsigned value, so guard the case first.
